title: Dog Scratching Ear Until It Bleeds: Causes and Care Guide
meta_desc: If your dog is scratching their ear until it bleeds, learn about the causes, urgency levels, and how to help your pet find immediate relief.
content_html:
TL;DR: A dog scratching its ear until it bleeds indicates significant pain or irritation from causes like infections or mites and requires a veterinary visit within 24 hours.

Why is my dog scratching their ear until it bleeds?

  • Intense localized irritation or pain often caused by an ear infection (otitis externa).
  • Underlying issues such as ear mites or allergies.
  • Foreign objects, like grass seeds, trapped inside the ear canal.
  • Repetitive scratching where the dog's claws break the sensitive skin on the ear flap or canal entrance.

Is it an emergency if my dog's ear is bleeding from scratching?

  • The urgency level for this condition is Medium; while rarely life-threatening, it indicates significant discomfort.
  • The condition will likely worsen without professional medical intervention.
  • You should schedule a veterinary appointment within 24 hours.
  • Seek immediate attention if there is a large, soft swelling on the ear flap, which may indicate an aural hematoma.

How does a photo help triage my dog's bleeding ear?

  • Allows a professional to see the color and consistency of any discharge.
  • Helps identify the degree of redness and the specific location of the trauma.
  • Determines whether the issue is a superficial skin wound or a deeper, more serious infection.
  • Provides visual information for a more accurate assessment of how quickly your dog needs to be seen.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Excessive ear scratching in dogs, often leading to bleeding, is commonly associated with pruritus stemming from conditions like atopic dermatitis or food allergies, where the ear canals are primary sites of allergic inflammation and secondary infection. These allergic conditions can predispose dogs to secondary bacterial or yeast otitis externa, potentially extending to the pinna. Head shaking and ear scratching can also lead to auricular hematomas, fluid-filled swellings on the concave surface of the pinna. Trauma can also directly cause hematoma development. Treatment of hematomas involves surgical drainage, flushing, and the placement of mattress sutures to eliminate the pocket.

Chapter: Surgery, Dermatology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 524)

faqs.question: Should I clean the blood out of my dog's ear?
faqs.answer: You can gently dab the outer ear flap with a clean, damp cloth to manage the bleeding, but avoid putting any liquids or cotton swabs down into the ear canal as this can cause further pain or damage the eardrum.

faqs.question: What if my dog is also shaking their head constantly?
faqs.answer: Frequent head shaking combined with scratching increases the risk of an aural hematoma. If the ear flap begins to look like a thick pillow, see a vet immediately to prevent permanent scarring.

faqs.question: Can I use human ear drops on my dog?
faqs.answer: No, you should never use human medications or over-the-counter drops without professional advice, as some ingredients can be toxic to dogs or harmful if the eardrum is ruptured.
